findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the AC #4 grep command to use POSIX ERE (`grep -Ern`) or split it into two separate grep commands (one for `AcliClient\.return_value\s*=`, one for `_FakeAcliModule`) so the verification is portable across GNU and BSD grep.
evidence: AC #4 states: `grep -rn 'AcliClient\(.return_value\)\?\s*=\|_FakeAcliModule' tests/ | grep -i acli` returns zero hits.
`\?` (optional quantifier) in GNU BRE matches zero-or-one occurrences; in POSIX BRE it matches a literal `?`.
`\|` (alternation) in GNU BRE matches either branch; in POSIX BRE it matches a literal `|`.

On macOS (BSD grep), both operators behave as literals — the command would match neither the `AcliClient.return_value = ...` form nor the `_FakeAcliModule` form correctly, and would silently return zero hits even on an unmigrated codebase.
findings.finding: The AC #4 migration-completeness grep command uses `\?` and `\|` operators that are GNU BRE extensions, not POSIX BRE — they are silently misinterpreted on macOS/BSD grep, making the 'zero hits = migration complete' gate unreliable on non-GNU platforms.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC #4 grep command: `grep -rn 'AcliClient\(\.return_value\)\?\s*=\|_FakeAcliModule' tests/ | grep -i acli`
scenarios: CI running on macOS or a non-GNU Linux image reports zero hits even when the migration is incomplete, giving a false green on the AC gate.
Developer running the verification locally on macOS passes the gate while several `AcliClient.return_value` seam doubles remain unmigrated.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the single grep with two portable commands: `grep -rn 'AcliClient\.return_value\s*=' tests/ | grep -i acli` and `grep -rn '_FakeAcliModule' tests/`, both expected to return zero hits. Or use `grep -Ern 'AcliClient(\.return_value)?\s*=|_FakeAcliModule' tests/ | grep -i acli` (note `-E` flag for ERE, with unescaped `(`, `)?`, `|`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite the AC4 grep to use `grep -E` (ERE) so the command is portable: `grep -rEn 'AcliClient(\.return_value)?\s*=|_FakeAcliModule' tests/ | grep -i acli` — and add a note that this must return zero hits to pass.
evidence: "grep -rn 'AcliClient\(\.return_value\)\?\s*=\|_FakeAcliModule' tests/ | grep -i acli returns zero hits"
Uses `\?` (BRE optional group) and `\|` (BRE alternation) without `-E` flag
findings.finding: The AC4 grep command `grep -rn 'AcliClient\(\.return_value\)\?\s*=\|_FakeAcliModule' tests/ | grep -i acli` uses BRE optional-group syntax (`\?`) and BRE alternation (`\|`) which are GNU grep extensions. On macOS/BSD CI runners or any non-GNU grep, `\?` is treated as a literal question mark and `\|` may not work as alternation — the command silently returns wrong results rather than erroring. This is a verify-command defect (taxonomy: wrong helper-script behavior / non-portable syntax).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC4 (the `_load_acli` seam migration completeness grep)
scenarios: CI runs on macOS (BSD grep) where the pattern compiles but matches only literal `\?` characters — the command returns zero hits vacuously on all runs, masking un-migrated doubles.
A developer runs the proving command locally on macOS and gets a false-clean result.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the grep to: `grep -rEn 'AcliClient(\.return_value)?[[:space:]]*=|_FakeAcliModule' tests/ | grep -i acli` (ERE via `-E`, POSIX character class for whitespace). Verify the pattern returns non-zero hits against the current codebase before the story starts, confirming it can catch un-migrated doubles.

findings.finding: The AC4 neutrality-gate grep command uses GNU-grep alternation syntax (`\|`) that is not supported by BSD grep (macOS, FreeBSD). On macOS the command silently produces no output regardless of matches, making the gate pass falsely (zero hits even when seam doubles still expose `AcliClient` factories).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC4: `grep -rn 'AcliClient\s*=\|_FakeAcliModule' tests/ | grep -i acli` as the migration-completeness verification gate
findings.suggested_fix: grep -rEn 'AcliClient[[:space:]]*=|_FakeAcliModule' tests/ | grep -i acli

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` has only ONE definition (in `outbound_links.py`; `acli_graph.py` has no such constant), so the task is a *relocation* to `adapters/jira/` followed by updating the import in `outbound_links.py`, not a de-duplication/merge — update the Context and Scope wording accordingly.
evidence: Plan: 'outbound_links.py:30-54 — a hand-copied duplicate of acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK ("keep in sync with acli_graph") — the duplicated link-constant leak'
grep of src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/acli_graph.py for '_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK': zero hits

outbound_links.py:30-35 comment: 'The canonical definition lives in acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK (Cycle 1), but the differ is loaded standalone via spec_from_file_location in tests' — the Cycle 1 definition was never created
Only occurrence in src/: outbound_links.py:42 — a single definition, not a duplicate
findings.finding: The plan characterises outbound_links.py:30-54's _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK as 'a hand-copied duplicate of acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK', implying a canonical definition already exists in acli_graph.py that the single-sourcing step will merge with. A grep of acli_graph.py returns zero hits for _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK — the 'canonical' copy does not exist. The outbound_links.py comment itself says 'canonical definition lives in acli_graph (Cycle 1)' which is aspirational text, not a live fact. An implementer following the plan will look for the acli_graph copy to merge/delete and find nothing, creating confusion about what 'single-sourcing' means concretely here. The action (move the sole definition to adapters/jira/) is architecturally correct but is a relocation, not a de-duplication.
findings.location: Context section — 'outbound_links.py:30-54 — a hand-copied duplicate of acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK'; Scope section — 'the outbound_links link-constant duplicate into adapters/jira/'
scenarios: Implementer searches acli_graph.py for the 'canonical' constant to determine which copy to keep and which to delete — finds nothing, unclear whether to delete outbound_links definition or just add a re-export.

AC#2 states 'grepping the core sites for those constructs returns zero hits' — if the only definition is in outbound_links.py and that is moved to adapters/jira/, the AC is trivially met by relocation alone, not de-duplication, which is fine but should be stated accurately.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a hand-copied duplicate of acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK' with 'a locally-declared constant (no package-import possible at standalone-load time) that should be relocated to adapters/jira/ and imported from there'. Remove the implication that an acli_graph canonical source exists.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the assertion in Context that `outbound_links.py:30-54` is 'a hand-copied duplicate of `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`' — grep confirms `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` does NOT exist in `acli_graph.py`; describe the task as moving the sole definition from `outbound_links.py` into `adapters/jira/` and updating the reference, not deduplicating two copies.
evidence: grep of src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/acli_graph.py returns zero hits for '_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK'
outbound_links.py:30 comment says 'The canonical definition lives in acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK (Cycle 1)' but the symbol does not exist there
Only definition: outbound_links.py:42 '_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK: dict[str, tuple[str, bool]] = {...}'
findings.finding: The plan asserts that outbound_links.py:30-54 holds 'a hand-copied duplicate of `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`', implying a canonical definition already exists in acli_graph.py that this story will single-source against. Grepping acli_graph.py finds zero occurrences of `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` — the constant is defined only in outbound_links.py itself (line 42). The comment inside outbound_links.py references 'acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK (Cycle 1)' but that symbol is absent from acli_graph.py. An executor following the plan will search for a canonical source in acli_graph.py that does not exist, potentially concluding the single-sourcing target is already correct or mis-locating the work. The AC2 instruction to 'relocate link constants into adapters/jira/' is directionally correct but the framing of 'duplicate of acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK' is a false premise.
findings.location: Context section — 'outbound_links.py:30-54 — a hand-copied duplicate of acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK'
scenarios: An executor reads the plan, searches acli_graph.py for the 'canonical' definition to understand the current state, finds nothing, and either questions the scope or mis-understands the work as 'removing a duplicate' when in fact there is only one definition to relocate.

The AC2 grep test ('grepping the core sites for those constructs returns zero hits') is still achievable, but the description of the task as 'single-sourcing' from a duplicate is misleading since there is no second copy in acli_graph.py.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Context bullet to: 'outbound_links.py:42-46 — the only definition of `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`; move it into `adapters/jira/` and import it from there (the comment at outbound_links.py:30 references acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK as a prior intent that was never realized — the symbol does not exist in acli_graph.py).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` no longer exists (the comment in `outbound_links.py:30` is stale); the task is simply relocating `outbound_links._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` — the only live copy — into `adapters/jira/`, not consolidating two copies.
evidence: grep of src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/acli_graph.py for '_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK' returns zero hits
outbound_links.py:30-35 comment: 'canonical definition lives in acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK (Cycle 1)' — stale comment, the symbol was removed from acli_graph
Plan AC2: 'outbound_links link constants are single-sourced under adapters/jira/' — but there is no second copy to consolidate; only one live copy exists
findings.finding: `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` does not exist in `acli_graph.py`: grep of `acli_graph.py` returns zero hits for `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`. The `outbound_links.py:30` comment says 'canonical definition lives in acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK (Cycle 1)' but the symbol is absent. The plan's Scope/AC2 frames this as 'single-sourcing' the link constants under `adapters/jira/`, implying `acli_graph` is the authoritative copy to consolidate from — but that source does not exist. This means `outbound_links._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` is the only live copy, not a duplicate.
findings.location: Context / Confirmed leak sites — 'outbound_links.py:30-54 — a hand-copied duplicate of acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK'
scenarios: Executor searches acli_graph.py for the constant to understand the 'canonical' shape before moving it to adapters/jira/, finds nothing, and is confused about what single-sourcing means here.
The plan's rationale ('keep in sync with acli_graph') is stale; the actual task is simply moving the only existing copy into adapters/jira/, which is different from eliminating a duplicate.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a hand-copied duplicate of acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK' with 'the sole live definition of the link-type map (the outbound_links.py comment references a now-absent acli_graph copy; move the live constant into adapters/jira/ and update the comment)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split the `apply_inbound_records.py` `"jira"` literal remediation into two parts: (a) the `:70`/`:74` provider/creation-channel strings passed to `rebar.ensure_identity_for`, which can be derived from a `RemoteRef.vendor`, and (b) the `:156` `validate_creation_channel("jira")` call, which enforces a reducer-layer closed vocabulary and cannot be replaced with `RemoteRef` without changing the reducer API — document whether `:156` is in or out of scope.
evidence: apply_inbound_records.py:154-156: `from rebar.reducer._version import validate_creation_channel` followed by `create_data['creation_channel'] = validate_creation_channel('jira')`
apply_inbound_records.py:70,74: `rebar.ensure_identity_for('jira', account_id, ..., creation_channel='jira')` — these ARE provider/channel strings
RemoteRef is a dataclass with `vendor`, `instance`, `remote_id` fields — it does not model a creation-channel key string
validate_creation_channel('jira') enforces a closed vocabulary at the reducer layer; replacing '"jira"' here with a RemoteRef attribute would require changes to the reducer contract
findings.finding: The plan's AC3 says to replace `apply_inbound_records.py`'s `"jira"` literals with `RemoteRef`/`IdentityConvention`, citing lines `:70`, `:74`, and `:156`. The `:156` call is `validate_creation_channel("jira")` — imported from `rebar.reducer._version`. This is a creation-channel vocabulary validator that accepts a fixed string key (`"jira"`), not a provider-identity concept. `RemoteRef`/`IdentityConvention` (which encode `vendor`/`instance`/`remote_id`) do not model creation-channel keys. Replacing a `validate_creation_channel("jira")` call with a `RemoteRef` would break the creation-channel contract enforced by the reducer layer.
findings.location: Context / Confirmed leak sites — 'apply_inbound_records.py:70,74,156 — the hardcoded "jira" provider/creation-channel literal'; AC3
scenarios: Executor tries to replace `validate_creation_channel("jira")` at :156 with a RemoteRef-based expression, finds no way to do so without changing the reducer API, and is blocked or makes a breaking change.

findings.finding: The plan asserts `outbound_links.py:30-54` holds 'a hand-copied duplicate of `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`', implying a canonical definition already exists in `acli_graph.py` that S4 will single-source from. Grep of `acli_graph.py` returns zero hits for `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`. The symbol does not exist there — the comment in `outbound_links.py:30` says 'canonical definition lives in acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK (Cycle 1)' but this refers to a past state; the canonical copy was apparently never committed or was removed. There is no existing canonical definition to delegate to in `adapters/jira/`.
findings.location: Context / Confirmed leak sites — `outbound_links.py:30-54`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a hand-copied duplicate of `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`' with 'the only definition of `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` in the codebase; this story creates the canonical copy under `adapters/jira/` and replaces the inline definition in `outbound_links.py` with an import from the adapter.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts `outbound_links.py:30-54` holds 'a hand-copied duplicate of `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`', implying a canonical definition exists in `acli_graph.py` that the plan will single-source by moving it to `adapters/jira/`. Grep of `acli_graph.py` returns zero matches for `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`. The constant exists only in `outbound_links.py` itself; `acli_graph.py` has no such symbol. The 'single-source' relocation is therefore not a de-duplication — it is a migration of the sole definition. The AC ('grepping the core sites for those constructs returns zero hits') is still achievable, but the executor will be confused by the 'keep in sync with acli_graph' framing when there is nothing in acli_graph to sync from.
findings.location: Context / Problem section — 'outbound_links.py:30-54 — a hand-copied duplicate of acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a hand-copied duplicate of acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK ("keep in sync with acli_graph")' with 'the sole definition of `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` (currently in `outbound_links.py:42`; the "keep in sync with acli_graph" comment is a historical note referencing a symbol that was never actually exported from `acli_graph.py`); the task is to move this definition into `adapters/jira/` so core no longer owns the link vocabulary'.

findings.finding: The plan asserts `outbound_links.py:30-54` holds 'a hand-copied duplicate of `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`', implying `acli_graph.py` is the canonical source that already defines this constant. Grep of `acli_graph.py` for 'RELATION' (and 'JIRA_LINK') returns zero matches — `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` does not exist in `acli_graph.py`. `outbound_links.py` is the ONLY definition. The plan's deduplication framing ('single-source the duplicate into adapters/jira/') is correct in spirit but incorrect in premise: there is no pre-existing canonical copy to consolidate from; the plan is migrating the sole copy to a new home.
findings.location: Context section, 'Confirmed leak sites', outbound_links.py:30-54 bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a hand-copied duplicate of `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`' with 'the sole definition of `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`, currently inlined in `outbound_links.py` with a stale comment saying "Keep in sync with acli_graph" — relocate it to `adapters/jira/` and import from there.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts `outbound_links.py:30-54` holds 'a hand-copied duplicate of `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`', implying a canonical definition exists in `acli_graph.py`. Grepping `acli_graph.py` returns zero hits for `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` — the symbol does not exist there. The single definition is in `outbound_links.py` itself, which only comments 'keep in sync with acli_graph'. There is no duplicate; there is one definition and a stale comment.
findings.location: Context section — 'Confirmed leak sites', outbound_links.py:30-54 bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the bullet with: '`outbound_links.py:30-54` — defines `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` with a stale comment claiming a canonical source in `acli_graph.py` that no longer exists; relocate the single definition into `adapters/jira/` and delete the comment.'

findings.finding: The plan describes `outbound_links.py`'s `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` as 'a hand-copied duplicate of `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`' that this story will single-source by moving the canonical to `adapters/jira/`. However, `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` does **not exist** in `acli_graph.py` — grep returns zero matches. The comment in `outbound_links.py:30` references it as the canonical location, but it was never defined there (or was removed). The constant in `outbound_links.py` is the only definition in the codebase. An executor expecting to find a canonical in `acli_graph` to reconcile against will find nothing and may introduce a wrong merge or create an unnecessary double-definition.
findings.location: Context section — 'Confirmed leak sites' / outbound_links.py:30-54; AC bullet 2
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a hand-copied duplicate of `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`' with 'the only definition of `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` (the `acli_graph` canonical referenced in `outbound_links.py:30` does not exist — move this definition to `adapters/jira/` and delete it from `outbound_links.py`)'.

findings.finding: The plan states `acli_subprocess.py` is 'an adapter-internal module' when prescribing `from rebar_reconciler.acli_subprocess import AssigneeNotFoundError`. In the current codebase `acli_subprocess.py` lives at `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/acli_subprocess.py` — at the root of the reconciler package, not under `adapters/jira/`. It is not adapter-internal; it is a core sibling module. This matters because the plan's own out-of-scope rule says physical file relocation is S5, so if `acli_subprocess.py` is expected to be adapter-internal at this story's point of execution, the plan contradicts itself.
findings.location: Approach — Exception-class catch site (`apply_handlers.py:181`) subsection
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'an adapter-internal module' to 'a reconciler-root module (to be moved to the adapter in S5)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit to the `run_differs.py` seam shape in the plan — e.g. state 'add a module-level `_load_transport()` function (matching the `_load_acli` pattern) that calls `select_backend(config).transport` and is patched via `patch.object(run_differs, "_load_transport", ...)` in the new test' — and remove the 'or' alternative.
evidence: "For `run_differs`, introduce a new seam (a `_load_acli`-equivalent or a backend-transport injection point)"
The AC only says 'gets a new seam/transport-injection point' — it does not pin which form
findings.finding: The `run_differs.py` seam design is left as an open executor choice: 'introduce a new seam (a `_load_acli`-equivalent **or** a backend-transport injection point)'. The two options have meaningfully different shapes (a local factory function vs. a DI parameter) that affect both the call-site code and the new test's patch target. An executor must make a real design decision the plan defers.
findings.location: Approach section, `run_differs.py:403` paragraph; also AC5
scenarios: Two executors independently implement this story and produce incompatible seam shapes, causing integration friction when S5 moves files.
The new test's patch target differs from what future stories expect, requiring re-migration.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'introduce a new seam (a `_load_acli`-equivalent or a backend-transport injection point)' with 'add a module-level `_load_transport(config)` function in `run_differs.py` that returns `select_backend(config).transport`; route the comment-client construction through it; patch it as `patch.object(run_differs, "_load_transport", return_value=fake_transport)` in the new test.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the Scope's migration count ('35 files — 30 patch.object + 5 monkeypatch.setattr') to match the Approach's enumeration of all four coverage groups: update Scope to read 'all three injection styles (~28 patch.object + ~5 monkeypatch.setattr + ~3 direct-attribute-assignment) plus the integration double (_fakes.py)' — or drop the numeric breakdown entirely from Scope and defer to AC 4 as the authoritative specification.
evidence: Scope: 'Update the _load_acli seam tests in lockstep with the seam contract change (all 35 files — 30 patch.object + 5 monkeypatch.setattr)'

Approach: 'three injection styles: patch.object(applier|fetcher, "_load_acli", …) (~28 files), monkeypatch.setattr(…, "_load_acli", …) (~5 files), and direct attribute assignment (mod._load_acli = lambda: … — e.g. conflict/test_parent_sync.py, mutate/test_comment_enrichment.py, mutate/test_link_sync.py)'
Approach: 'Plus the integration double at tests/integration/rebar_reconciler/jira_contract/_fakes.py'
AC 4: 'across all three injection styles (patch.object, monkeypatch.setattr, direct attribute assignment) and the integration _fakes.py double'
The Scope's '35 = 30 + 5' omits the ~3 direct-assignment files and _fakes.py, understating the migration surface by ~4 files and missing a whole style category
findings.finding: The Scope section states '35 files — 30 patch.object + 5 monkeypatch.setattr' for the _load_acli seam migration, but the Approach section explicitly enumerates a third injection style — direct attribute assignment (~3 files: conflict/test_parent_sync.py, mutate/test_comment_enrichment.py, mutate/test_link_sync.py) — plus the integration double (_fakes.py), neither of which is included in the '35 = 30 + 5' breakdown. AC item 4 correctly says 'all three injection styles ... and the integration _fakes.py double,' putting Scope and AC in direct contradiction about what is covered.
findings.location: Scope section ('Update the _load_acli seam tests in lockstep… across all 35 files — 30 patch.object + 5 monkeypatch.setattr'); Approach section (three injection styles + _fakes.py enumeration); AC item 4
scenarios: An implementer following the Scope's '35 files' count would stop after patching the patch.object and monkeypatch.setattr sites, leaving the three direct-assignment files and _fakes.py with module-shaped doubles — the neutrality grep in AC 4 would then still return hits, blocking sign-off.
A reviewer validating completeness against the Scope count would incorrectly conclude migration is done with 35 files migrated, missing the fourth injection style.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope bullet, replace '(all **35** files — 30 `patch.object` + 5 `monkeypatch.setattr`)' with '(all **three** injection styles: `patch.object` (~28 files), `monkeypatch.setattr` (~5 files), direct attribute assignment (~3 files: `conflict/test_parent_sync.py`, `mutate/test_comment_enrichment.py`, `mutate/test_link_sync.py`), **plus** the integration double `_fakes.py` — see AC 4 for the authoritative completion gate).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `config` reaches the zero-arg `_load_acli()` seam in both `applier.py` and `fetcher.py` — e.g. confirm the intended pattern is `load_config()` called inside the seam body (mirroring `acli_subprocess.py:71`'s `load_config().reconciler.jira_cli_timeout` precedent), and note whether memoization is needed to avoid redundant config reads per pass.
evidence: Plan states: 'post-change, `_load_acli()` returns the selected backend's **`TicketTransport` object directly** (i.e. `select_backend(config).transport`)' — `config` appears in pseudocode but no acquisition path is specified for the zero-arg seam
Contrast: `_attestation.py` AC says 'route through `select_backend(load_config()).transport`' — config acquisition IS specified there but not for the seam
`applier.py:224` and `fetcher.py:113` are both `def _load_acli():` (zero-arg) — signature change would break ~35 patch.object call sites
findings.finding: The plan states `_load_acli()` should return `select_backend(config).transport` but never specifies how `config` reaches the zero-arg seam function. Since `_load_acli()` is defined as a zero-arg function in both `applier.py:224` and `fetcher.py:113`, the only options are (a) call `load_config()` inside `_load_acli()`, (b) change the signature (which would break all `patch.object` call sites), or (c) use a module-level cached config. Option (a) is the natural fit given S3's pattern (`acli_subprocess.py` already calls `load_config().reconciler.jira_cli_timeout`), but the plan leaves this unspecified. An implementer could reasonably choose a memoized closure, a bare `load_config()` call, or even a singleton — each with different test-isolation implications. For integration tests that patch `_load_acli`, config setup is bypassed, so the choice only matters for production and `_attestation.py` (which the plan does specify calls `select_backend(load_config())` explicitly). The absence of this spec leaves the implementer to guess.
findings.location: Approach section — `_load_acli` seam new return-value contract; also AC line for `_attestation.py`
scenarios: Implementer adds `load_config()` call inside `_load_acli()` — correct but not stated; each `apply` or `fetch_snapshot` call now hits disk/env for config, which may be slow or break tests that don't set up TOML fixtures
Implementer treats config as a module-level singleton — introduces cross-test state leakage similar to the registry pattern (which the plan explicitly guards with `_reset_registry_for_test`)
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Approach section under the `_load_acli` contract: 'The updated `_load_acli()` body calls `load_config()` internally (the same pattern `acli_subprocess.py` already uses at line :71) — no signature change; test doubles patch out `_load_acli` entirely so no config fixture is required for existing tests.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the post-change disposition of `BatchApplyContext.acli` in the Approach or Scope section: state whether the field is (a) removed from the dataclass (and confirm no other handler reads it), (b) repurposed to hold the `TicketTransport` and renamed accordingly, or (c) retained as `None` — and update the AC to assert the chosen shape.
evidence: Plan states: 'ctx.acli/BatchApplyContext is updated so no other site reads an exception class off the transport' — but does not say whether the `acli` field is removed, replaced with the transport, or deprecated.
Current code: `BatchApplyContext` at apply_handlers.py:59 has `acli: Any`; `applier.py:654` sets `acli=acli` where `acli` is the module returned by `_load_acli()`.

Post-change, `_load_acli()` returns a bare `TicketTransport` — passing that as `ctx.acli` changes the field's semantics silently. Whether the field is retained (holding the transport), removed (requiring callers to use `ctx.client`), or replaced is not specified.
AC #7 only asserts the `grep` for `ctx.acli.AssigneeNotFoundError` returns zero hits — it does not specify the resulting shape of `BatchApplyContext`.
findings.finding: `BatchApplyContext.acli` field disposition after the seam change is unspecified: the plan says 'ctx.acli/BatchApplyContext is updated so no other site reads an exception class off the transport' but never defines what `ctx.acli` becomes (removed from the dataclass, replaced with the transport object, set to None, or renamed). This is a decision rule that must be co-located with the `BatchApplyContext` schema (`apply_handlers.py:59`), and its absence leaves an executor needing to invent the answer.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Exception-class catch site (apply_handlers.py:181)' paragraph; apply_handlers.py:59 (BatchApplyContext dataclass); applier.py:654 (ctx = BatchApplyContext(..., acli=acli, ...))
scenarios: An executor removes `ctx.acli` from BatchApplyContext entirely, breaking any other handler that uses it for non-exception-class purposes.

An executor retains `ctx.acli` holding the transport, silently changing its semantic contract without updating the dataclass docstring, leaving a confusing `acli: Any` field that now holds a transport.
A future story expects `ctx.acli` to carry the acli module and breaks because this story left it holding a transport.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Exception-class catch site' paragraph: 'Remove `acli: Any` from `BatchApplyContext` (grep `ctx.acli` in `apply_handlers.py` confirms `apply_handlers.py:181` is the only reader; after the rewrite to a direct import it becomes dead). Update `applier.py:654` to omit the `acli=` kwarg. Add a grep assertion to AC #7: `grep -rn "ctx\.acli" src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/apply_handlers.py` returns zero hits.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the ~5 test files that set `fake_acli_mod.AssigneeNotFoundError = …` as part of the `_load_acli` seam migration scope, confirm these lines are dropped when the double is repointed to a transport shape (since `apply_handlers.py` will import the exception directly), and add a completion-grep for `AssigneeNotFoundError` attribute assignment on seam doubles to AC4.
evidence: tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/apply/test_applier_assignee_soft_fail.py:169: fake_acli_mod.AssigneeNotFoundError = acli_mod.AssigneeNotFoundError
tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/apply/test_transition_error_soft_fail.py:137: fake_acli_mod.AssigneeNotFoundError = acli_mod.AssigneeNotFoundError
tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/apply/test_applier_stale_binding_404.py:123: fake.AssigneeNotFoundError = acli_mod.AssigneeNotFoundError
tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/apply/test_applier_comment_error_surfaced.py:93: fake.AssigneeNotFoundError = acli_mod.AssigneeNotFoundError
Plan says apply_handlers.py:181 is rewritten to import AssigneeNotFoundError directly — so transport-shaped doubles no longer need the attribute
AC4 grep pattern does not cover 'fake.AssigneeNotFoundError =' leftover lines
findings.finding: Tests that build `_load_acli` seam doubles for the `AssigneeNotFoundError` path (e.g., `test_applier_assignee_soft_fail.py:169`, `test_transition_error_soft_fail.py:137`, `test_applier_stale_binding_404.py:123`) set `fake_acli_mod.AssigneeNotFoundError = acli_mod.AssigneeNotFoundError` on their module-shaped fake — supplying the exception class through the seam. After the plan's change, `apply_handlers.py:181` imports `AssigneeNotFoundError` directly and no longer reads it off the transport; these test files' `fake_acli_mod.AssigneeNotFoundError = …` lines become dead code. But more critically: these files are in the `_load_acli` seam migration set (35-file set), and once repointed to a transport-shaped double, the transport double should NOT carry an `AssigneeNotFoundError` attribute — yet the AC4 grep completion check (`grep -rn 'AcliClient(…' | grep -i acli`) would not catch leftover `fake.AssigneeNotFoundError = …` lines, leaving inconsistent test doubles.
findings.location: Approach — test-double migration surface; AC4 migration-completeness grep
scenarios: Seam-double migration leaves ~5 test files with `fake.AssigneeNotFoundError = acli_mod.AssigneeNotFoundError` lines that are now dead code on a transport-shaped double — the migration is incomplete but the AC4 gate doesn't catch it.
Or worse: a developer removes those lines thinking they are dead, but a different catch site still relies on the transport carrying the class.

The Scope section and Acceptance Criteria make no mention of updating the ADR.
The stale ADR will persist after this story ships, with the corrected leak-site list living only in this plan's prose rather than in the canonical ADR.

findings.location: Context / Problem section — 'the ADR §(a) list is stale — it names `baseline_shadow.py`, which does not exist, and omits `apply_inbound.py`'
scenarios: A future engineer reads the ADR to understand the leak-site inventory, finds `baseline_shadow.py` listed, searches for it, finds it doesn't exist, and wastes time debugging the discrepancy.
S5's author consults the ADR §(a) list for which files to physically relocate and acts on the stale inventory.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'Update ADR §(a) to remove the non-existent `baseline_shadow.py` entry and add `apply_inbound.py`, reflecting the verified 12-site inventory.' Add a corresponding AC: '`grep -rn baseline_shadow` in the ADR returns zero hits.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the apply_handlers.py → acli_subprocess coupling explicitly (e.g., re-export AssigneeNotFoundError from the Backend port/shared exceptions module so apply_handlers imports from the port, not adapter internals) and extend the neutrality gate to also assert zero `acli_subprocess` imports in core modules outside adapters/.
evidence: 'import the class directly — from rebar_reconciler.acli_subprocess import AssigneeNotFoundError (defined at acli_subprocess.py:147, an adapter-internal module)'
The story's stated goal is that 'the core stops importing adapters.jira.* / the root vendor modules' — apply_handlers now imports from acli_subprocess which is noted as adapter-internal
No mitigation (re-export from port, shared exceptions module) is proposed; the coupling is introduced as a direct import with no further discussion
findings.finding: The plan routes `apply_handlers.py` to import `AssigneeNotFoundError` directly from `acli_subprocess` (described as 'an adapter-internal module'), creating a new direct dependency from a core-adjacent module into adapter internals — the inverse of the layering enforcement this story performs elsewhere — without justifying this as a permitted exception to the layering rule or specifying a mitigation (e.g., re-exporting the exception from the Backend port or a shared exceptions module).
findings.location: Approach section — Exception-class catch site (apply_handlers.py:181); Scope section; AC for apply_handlers
scenarios: The layering neutrality grep/test gate checks for adapters.jira.* and root vendor modules but does NOT check for acli_subprocess imports in core modules — the new coupling is invisible to the gate.
When acli_subprocess is relocated in S5 or the adapter is replaced with a non-ACLI transport, apply_handlers.py will silently break because it bypasses the port.
findings.suggested_fix: Define `AssigneeNotFoundError` (or re-export it) in the `Backend` port or a shared `rebar_reconciler.exceptions` module, have `acli_subprocess` inherit/alias from there, and update `apply_handlers.py` to import from the port/shared module. Extend the neutrality grep to: `grep -rn 'from rebar_reconciler.acli' src/.../rebar_reconciler/ --include='*.py' | grep -v adapters/`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for the `_load_acli` seam contract change — either describe an expand-contract intermediate step (e.g., a thin adapter object satisfying both the old module interface and the new TicketTransport interface during transition), or name an explicit back-out commit strategy if characterization tests go RED mid-migration.
evidence: 'Update the _load_acli seam tests in lockstep with the seam contract change (all 35 files)'
'evolve the seam + its patch.object tests'

No mention of an expand-contract shim (e.g., returning an object that satisfies both the old module-shaped interface and the new transport interface during transition), nor of a named back-out commit/branch strategy if characterization tests fail mid-migration.
T4 criterion: 'The remedy for a destructive/breaking change is an explicit ROLLBACK / back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing — checking only that breakage is acknowledged is insufficient.'
findings.finding: The plan changes the `_load_acli` seam contract (return value changes from an acli module to a bare TicketTransport) across ~35 test files atomically, but provides no explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing if the migration breaks mid-flight.
findings.location: Approach section — '(1) applier/fetcher — evolve their existing _load_acli seams'; Scope — 'Update the _load_acli seam tests in lockstep with the seam contract change (all 35 files)'
scenarios: Migration stalls mid-way (e.g., after applier seam is changed but before fetcher/run_differs are updated) — the codebase is in a broken intermediate state with no described recovery path.
A characterization test reveals an unanticipated behavioral difference after the seam change — the team has no explicit back-out procedure to follow.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-paragraph rollback note: 'If characterization tests fail mid-migration, revert to the pre-change seam by [git revert / feature-branch strategy]. Alternatively, introduce a transitional wrapper object that exposes both `.AcliClient(...)` and the TicketTransport methods so both old and new call sites work until all 35 files are migrated, then remove the shim in a follow-up.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add 'update the Backend-port ADR (or AGENTS.md) to reflect the corrected _load_acli contract (returns TicketTransport directly, not a module), the TicketTransport surface split by path, and the stale §(a) leak-site list correction' to Scope and add a corresponding grep/doc-diff AC.
evidence: 'the ADR §(a) list is stale — it names baseline_shadow.py, which does not exist, and omits apply_inbound.py'
ABSENCE: no 'update ADR' or 'update AGENTS.md' item appears in Scope, Tasks, or Acceptance Criteria
The _load_acli contract change (from returning a module to returning a TicketTransport) is a novel architectural decision affecting ~35 test files and 4 production transport sites
findings.finding: The plan notes the ADR §(a) list is stale and makes significant architectural decisions (changing the _load_acli contract to return a TicketTransport directly, defining the TicketTransport surface for fetcher vs. applier paths, replacing the module-shaped double pattern) but does NOT include updating or creating an ADR/AGENTS.md/design doc as a scope item or acceptance criterion.
findings.location: Scope section and Approach section (no ADR update task listed)
scenarios: A future developer looks for the canonical _load_acli contract definition and finds only the stale ADR, leading to incorrect assumptions about what the seam returns and how to write test doubles for new transport sites.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Scope bullet: 'Update ADR [rebar:ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798] §(a) leak-site list and add a §(b) section documenting the new _load_acli contract (returns TicketTransport, not acli module) and the rationale for splitting the transport surface by path (fetcher vs. applier).' Add a corresponding AC: 'The ADR reflects the corrected leak-site list and the _load_acli contract change — verified by a doc diff.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit prerequisite guard: state that this story's implementation tasks (specifically the `_load_acli` body rewrite and AC5/AC6) cannot begin until S2 (ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798) and S3 (94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75) are merged and `select_backend(config).transport` is importable and matches the signatures assumed here.
evidence: grep of entire src/rebar tree for 'select_backend' returns zero hits
grep of entire src/rebar tree for 'TicketTransport' returns zero hits
S2 ticket ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798 status: open; signature manifest includes 'dep absent src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_backend.py'
S3 ticket 94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75 status: open; signature manifest includes 'dep absent src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_backend_registry.py'
findings.finding: The plan's Approach and multiple ACs rely on `select_backend(config).transport` and `TicketTransport` as already-callable symbols — e.g. 'post-change, `_load_acli()` returns the selected backend's `TicketTransport` object directly (i.e. `select_backend(config).transport`)'. Both `select_backend` and `TicketTransport` are absent from the current codebase (`grep` across all of `src/rebar` returns zero hits for either symbol). The prerequisite tickets S2 (ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798) and S3 (94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75) are both `status: open` with plan-review signatures confirming `dep absent` for `_backend.py`, `_backend_registry.py`, and `adapters/jira/backend.py`. While the plan correctly names them as prerequisites, the implementation steps describe routing calls through `select_backend(config).transport` as if this API surface is settled — but its exact signature is only defined in the S2/S3 specs, which have not been implemented. If either S2 or S3 lands with a different `transport` attribute name, accessor pattern, or config argument, this story's rewire tasks are directly broken.
findings.location: Approach section — 'post-change, `_load_acli()` returns the selected backend's **`TicketTransport` object directly** (i.e. `select_backend(config).transport`)'; also AC5, AC6
scenarios: S2 lands and defines `Backend.transport` as a property returning a named type, but the type is renamed or the access pattern changes — this story's seam rewire breaks at the `_load_acli` body.
S3 lands and `select_backend(config)` takes a different argument than the app-level `Config` (e.g. takes `ReconcilerConfig` directly) — every transport site in this story breaks.
S2/S3 are not merged before this story is claimed, making it impossible to implement without deviation from the plan.

findings.finding: AC4's migration completeness grep (`'AcliClient\s*=\|_FakeAcliModule'`) misses the MagicMock `.return_value` assignment pattern — `fake_acli_mod.AcliClient.return_value = fake_client` — used in at least 8 applier test files (test_transition_error_soft_fail.py, test_applier_comment_error_surfaced.py, test_applier_stale_binding_404.py, test_applier_assignee_soft_fail.py, test_silent_noop_canary.py, test_applier_cross_project_guard.py, test_applier_create_comment_error_surfaced.py, test_fail_loud_comment_errors.py). These are module-shaped doubles that the gate is intended to catch. The grep could return zero hits while those doubles remain module-shaped, giving a false green on migration completeness.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC4 — grep clause `grep -rn 'AcliClient\s*=\|_FakeAcliModule' tests/ | grep -i acli`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC4 grep with: `grep -rn 'AcliClient\(\.return_value\)\?\s*=' tests/ | grep -i acli` — or more precisely, run two greps: the existing one plus `grep -rn 'AcliClient\.return_value' tests/` and assert both return zero hits in scope.

findings.finding: The plan lists `_attestation.py:82` as a transport/construction site requiring rewire (Scope + Approach) but provides no dedicated AC, no seam test description, and no decision on whether a new seam or a direct injection point is introduced — unlike `run_differs.py` which explicitly calls for a new seam + new test. An executor cannot determine whether a test is expected for `_attestation.py`'s rewire or whether it is considered covered by AC1's neutrality grep alone.
findings.location: Approach — '_attestation.py:82' paragraph; Scope bullet 1; Acceptance Criteria (all ACs)
